texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We propose MemoChat , a pipeline for refining instructions that enables large language models (LLMs) to effectively employ self-composed memos for maintaining consistent long-range open-domain conversations. We demonstrate a long-range open-domain conversation through iterative ' memorization-retrieval-response ' cycles. This requires us to carefully design tailored tuning instructions for each distinct stage. The instructions are reconstructed from a collection of public datasets to teach the LLMs to memorize and retrieve past dialogues with structured memos, leading to enhanced consistency when participating in future conversations. We invite experts to manually annotate a test set designed to evaluate the consistency of long-range conversations questions. Experiments on three testing scenarios involving both open-source and API-accessible chatbots at scale verify the efficacy of MemoChat , which outperforms strong baselines 1 .

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Large language models (LLMs) have brought about a substantial revolution, fundamentally changing our lifestyle. They have emerged as a new platform that connects the realms of academia and industry within the field of artificial intelligence (Zhao et al., 2023b; Yang et al., 2023). In particular, LLMs have demonstrated an enhanced ability to synchronize effectively with human cognitive processes (Du et al., 2022; Taori et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023c; Ouyang et al., 2022) and consequently have served as a foundation towards creating human-like conversational dialogues.

texts.orig: 2023). Figure 1 illustrates an example of such long-range conversations with more than 20 exchanges between a human user and a chat bot. It involves several sequential topics such as ' Quantum Physics ' (1 to 8 turns), ' Business Etiquette in Japan ' (11 to 14 turns) and ' Polynomial Math Problem ' (16 to 18 turns), while other topics are simply referred to as ' MULTIPLE TURNS '. Also, two topics-related long-range questions are raised subsequently, including one for evoking memories associated with ' Business Etiquette in Japan ', and the other for retrieving historical information pertaining to ' Quantum Physics '. Such long-range open-domain conversations that involve diverse topics presents a challenge for conventional methods, as they struggle to effectively address the issue of retaining contextual coherence over long stretches of discourse. One possible strategy is to expand the input text window (Tworkowski et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023; Ding et al., 2023). For example, positional interpolation (Chen et al., 2023) was presented to empower RoPE-based (Su et al., 2022) LLMs with a text window that is 600 times longer than its original pretraining version. Unfortunately, the window length can not be arbitrar- ily expanded (Beltagy et al., 2020; Kitaev et al., 2020). Another potential solution is to resort to additional memories for tracking the history of conversations (Zhong et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2023a). For example, MPC was proposed (Lee et al., 2023) to regularly store persona summaries of recent conversations as history, then use new user query Q to retrieve the top-K history D via an external DPR retriever (Karpukhin et al., 2020), and finally generate a new response based on both Q and D . Similarly, an event and user portrait memory managed with FAISS (Johnson et al., 2019) were adopted by (Zhong et al., 2023). Despite the advantage in retaining infinite contextual inputs, compared with text window extension, memory mechanism may cause accumulation errors during the retrieval of past evidence (Xu et al., 2022b). As a result, how to enhance the accuracy of memory recall and improve the response consistency of LLM-powered chatbots in long-range open-domain conversation remains unsolved (Xu et al., 2022a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we are particularly interested in building memory-enhanced chatbots. Instead of replying on external tools for memory creation and recall (Lee et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2023; Hu et al., 2023; Kynoch and Latapie, 2023), we propose a streamlined approach where we eliminate the need for these complex associate modules. Instead, we create a simplified pipeline that solely utilizes LLMs to power chatbots. The proposed memo-equipped pipeline, MemoChat , aims for guiding LLMs to use simple on-the-fly memos for maintaining consistent long-range open-domain conversation. We base our approach on the concept of ' memorization-retrieval-response ' loops within the context of long-range open-domain conversations. To achieve this, except for zero-shot learning with chatbots accessed via public APIs (OpenAI, 2023; Google, 2023), we reconstruct a series of instructions from publicly available datasets (Adlakha et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2021; Ghosal et al., 2023) to fine-tune open-source LLM-powered chatbots. This fine-tuning process helps the chatbots familiarize themselves with each stage within the loop, and employ structured memos for maintaining a consistent long-range conversation. For fair evaluation, we invite experts to manually annotate a testing set specifically designed to evaluate the consistency of long-range conversations across three different types of testing scenarios. We verify the effectiveness of the proposed MemoChat through a comprehensive evaluation involving both a chatbot accessible via public API and four different opensource LLMs. This evaluation is carried out using a very strong LLM judge (Zheng et al., 2023). The contributions of this paper are listed as follows:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 Long-Range Open-Domain Conversation
texts.text: 2.1 Long-Range Open-Domain Conversation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Long-range open-domain conversation has been a widely explored task recently. Doc2Dial, a goaloriented document-grounded dialogue dataset is proposed by (Feng et al., 2020), including around 48k long-range conversations with an average of 14 turns. MultiSessionChat is a dataset concerning one long-range conversation over multiple sessions (Xu et al., 2022a). While most of these traditional datasets focus on few-topic human-human scenarios, there has been a proliferation on newly multitopic human-chatbot scenarios (Kim et al., 2023).
texts.text: Long-range open-domain conversation has been a widely explored task recently. Doc2Dial, a goaloriented document-grounded dialogue dataset is proposed by (Feng et al., 2020), including around 48k long-range conversations with an average of 14 turns. MultiSessionChat is a dataset concerning one long-range conversation over multiple sessions (Xu et al., 2022a). While most of these traditional datasets focus on few-topic human-human scenarios, there has been a proliferation on newly multitopic human-chatbot scenarios (Kim et al., 2023).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The challenges of long-range open-domain conversations lie on the answerability of open-domain questions and the capacity of long-range input understanding. External knowledge graph was incorporated to improve the answer ability of out-ofdistribution open-domain questions (Zhou et al., 2020). With the development of LLMs, LLMpowered chatbots have largely improved their answering ability (Shuster et al., 2022b,a). On the other hand, various strategies have been proposed to tackle the challenge of long-range input understanding. One possible strategy is to use additional memory to filter shorter key facts in the past longrange history to replace it. MPC (Lee et al., 2023) and MemoryBank (Zhong et al., 2023) were proposed to use additional memory and external retriever to manage persona, event or user portrait information. Our work differs from existing studies in that we solely teach the chatbot to self-use memos, and we focus on summarizing all facts in the history instead of partial persona or event facts.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Instruction Tuning
texts.text: 2.2 Instruction Tuning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Instruction tuning is a widely used strategy to fine-tune LLMs on tasks with meta-instruction ahead (Wei et al., 2022a). The effectiveness of instruction tuning has been proved on many tasks, including text reasoning (Sanh et al., 2022), humanbot alignment (Ouyang et al., 2022), document ranking (Qin et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023), document retrieval (Mueller et al., 2023), and general conversation ability (Gupta et al., 2022). In this work, we spend low-cost yet carefully design instructions to teach LLM-powered chatbots for selfusing on-the-fly memo to strengthen the response consistency within long-range chatting stream.

texts.orig: Figure 2 depicts the overall framework of our proposed MemoChat pipeline, which assigns the chatbot with a ' memorization-retrieval-response ' loop of inner thinking. In the left of Figure 2, the user starts with the topic of ' Quantum Physics ', then ends with ' Business Etiquette ' topic after multiple turns of the conversation. Very different from traditional methods that retrieve directly on these accumulated dialogues, the chatbot under our MemoChat , as shown on the upper-right of Figure 2, will automatically builds and updates a structured on-the-fly memo, storing past dialogues in categories. Then, the retrieval is conducted over all recordings according to their topics and summaries. In the bottom-left, Figure 2 illustrates an occurrence in which the chatbot is required to link the concept of ' Quantum Physics ' aforementioned in the very beginning of the conversation. The chatbot manages to recall related evidences, and generate a consistent answer in bottom-right Figure 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Instruction Structure . ' Memorization-retrievalresponse ' mechanism stores the past dialogues in memo x m , recalls query-related evidences x h ′ from memo, and generates response ˆ Y based on retrieved evidences. Different from previous literature (Lee et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2023) requiring additional complex modules, we reorganize the dialogue histories into structured on-the-fly memos, such that the basic LLM-powered chatbots can simultaneously handle the memo. Thus, we develop three instructions for each stage as shown in Figure 3. Overall, all instructions follow similar structure: (1) description of each stage task ( green area ); (2) main body of task-oriented inputs ( bisque area ); (3) explanation of each task ( yellow area ). The combination of three serves as the input prompts of the chatbot and ground-truth answers are provided ( blue area ) for fine-tuning.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Herein, the 'Memo Writing' task asks the chatbot to read a task conversation, partition the conversation based on all possible topics, write short summaries for every sub-dialogues, and write the above results to the memo in a JSON format with each instance including keys of 'topic', 'summary' and 'dialogues'. We consolidate the task explanation with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) (Wei et al., 2022b) and In-Context Learning (ICL) (Dong et al., 2023). The 'Memo Retrieval' task asks the chatbot to read a user query and a series of topic options, select out one or more related options and report option ID with a special format. Inspired by previous literature (Rajpurkar et al., 2018), one ' None of the others (NOTO) ' option is randomly inserted in the candidate options, given that the user may arbitrarily launch a new topic. To support a fast implementation, we retrieve related contents only upon the 'topics' and 'summaries' from the on-thefly memo. Finally, the 'Chat w/ Memo' task feeds the latest query, prefixed with retrieved evidences and recent dialogues, to the chatbot. With these related evidences, the chatbot can now produce more consistent response even in the long-range open-domain conversations.

texts.orig: TopicoQA covers 3.9k conversations that involve 4 topics and 13 turns of dialogue on average. DialogSum covers 13k conversations that focus on 1 topic across 5.5 turns on average, and provides manual summary for each conversation. Both TopicoQA and DialogSum use human-human conversations in spoken style, and focus on daily life scenes. While Alpaca-GPT4 is a human-chatbot 1-turn conversation dataset created by running GPT4 API (OpenAI, 2023) on the Alpaca dataset (Taori et al., 2023). It covers more sophisticated topics in written style. We sample 1.6k cases to balance the instructions from another two datasets.
texts.text: TopicoQA covers 3.9k conversations that involve 4 topics and 13 turns of dialogue on average. DialogSum covers 13k conversations that focus on 1 topic across 5.5 turns on average, and provides manual summary for each conversation. Both TopicoQA and DialogSum use human-human conversations in spoken style, and focus on daily life scenes. While Alpaca-GPT4 is a human-chatbot 1-turn conversation dataset created by running GPT4 API (OpenAI, 2023) on the Alpaca dataset (Taori et al., 2023). It covers more sophisticated topics in written style. We sample 1.6k cases to balance the instructions from another two datasets.

texts.orig: Table 1 reports the statistics and sources of the three instruction training sets. The 'Memo Writing' set consists of 1,790 instances from TopicoQA and 1,256 samples from DialogSum. Given the unavailability of 'summary' information, we only construct 'topic' and 'dialogues' in the memo for conversations from TopicoQA dataset. Considering only one topic in each conversation of DialogSum, we combine a few variable set of conversations to mimic a multi-topic long-range conversation. Similar strategies are adopted to form 'Memo Retrieval' set that contains 1,719 and 1,935 instructions from TopicoQA and DialogSum, respectively. For each retrieval instruction, its query sentence is made up with several sequential sentences from original conversation and related topics are extracted as options. The ' NOTO ' option is inserted with a probability of 10%. The 'Chat w/ Memo' set comprises 1,602 and 1,698 instructions from DialogSum and Alpaca-GPT4. The former aims at generating instructions with related evidences, and the latter generates instructions without any related evidences to fit with unseen topics.

texts.orig: Challenges . To our best knowledge, we are the first to build such a group of systematic sequential instructions. We report major challenges for any potential future follow-ups. Some specific examples are available in Appendix C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first challenge faceed is 'Prompt Copy' in which the LLMs found a special shortcut to directly copy format examples in the prompts as the answer. To avoid this, we suggest to use dummy variables to replace any numerical values to avoid possible answer leaking. The second challenge refers to 'Catastrophic Forgetting' in which the chatbots lean towards generating early-stopped or repeated answers if the volume of 'Chat w/ Memo' is less than 'Memo Writing' or 'Memo Retrieval'. A balanced volume is encouraged by mixing with certain amount of dialogue corpus to maintain the chatting ability (Ghosal et al., 2023). We consider Alpaca-GPT4 in our construction. The third challenge lies in 'Prompt Misplacement' in which the chatbots performance degrade when swapping the input order of the main body of task-oriented inputs ( bisque area in Figure 3) and explanation of each task ( yellow area in Figure 3). Task explanation attached at the end is crucial for chatbots to understand instructions better (Liu et al., 2023b).

texts.orig: Datasets . Except for the reconstructed instruction datasets, we also invite three experts to manually annotate a new long-range open-domain conversation dataset to validate the response consistency. The annotated dataset, referred to as MT-Bench+, is built based on MT-Bench (Zheng et al., 2023), a human-chatbot testing dataset with a total of 80 two-turn open-domain questions, covering topics such as 'STEM exams' and 'literary writing'. This is achieved by: (1) expanding each of the twoturn questions to a four-turn version by the experts; (2) sampling 2 ∼ 4 questions from every 3 ∼ 4 of four-turn versions to form a long-range chatting stream with a total of 12 ∼ 15 turns; (3) adding longrange questions to the end of the chatting streams for 'Retrospection', 'Continuation' and 'Conjunction' by the experts. Herein, 'Retrospection' is restricted to the aforementioned terms related ques- tions ( e.g. , span extraction), 'Continuation' makes a further task based on previous knowledge ( e.g. , story completion), and 'Conjunction' is to ask a cross-topic response ( e.g. , redo task 2 in reference to task 1). We build 18 questions for each of the three types in MT-Bench+, 54 in total.

texts.orig: Baselines . Weconsider the popular Fastchat-T5-3b and Vicuna-7b/13b/33b (Zheng et al., 2023), which are human-aligned versions of Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022) and Llama (Touvron et al., 2023) with massive human-bot dialogues (Kim et al., 2023), respectively, as the baseline models. We further finetune them on our reconstructed instruction data to form our MemoChat . For all experiments, we set the text window to 2k to follow off-the-shelf conventions (Workshop, 2023). However, our MemoChat keeps the flexibility and scalability to larger windows as chatbots can automatically adjust memory range. As for downstream consistency testing, one public API and two open-source memory-assistant chatbots are included as baselines:

texts.orig: GPT4 API is excluded as GPT4-generated data are included in the training set, and we rely on GPT4 for evaluation in downstream chatting test. Metrics . We adopt four groups of metrics to evaluate the performance of intermediate instruction tuning and the downstream consistency checking. Since we ask chatbots to partition dialogue histories to build memo, in reference to Name Entity Recognition (NER) (Ding et al., 2021), we therefore evaluate generated topics and summaries in 'MemoWriting' task with precision ( P ), recall ( R ) and micro F1-score ( F 1 ) only when predicted subsession range and ground truth matches. In detail, the P score of topic checking uses exact match following NER, while the P score of summary checking is replaced with float BertScore (Zhang et al., 2020) number varying between 0 to 1. We use regular F1-score ( F 1 ) on 'Memo Retrieval', and use BertScore to check the semantic similarity between generated response and golden references in 'Chat w/ Memo' task. As for downstream response consistency checking, inspired by LLMas-a-judge strategy (Zheng et al., 2023), we select out related dialogue histories, and the long-range checking question with corresponding response, then use GPT4 to score the faithfulness with an integer scale 1 ∼ 100. It has been explored in (Pan et al., 2023) to show that credible LLMs such as GPT4 have a comparable analysis ability to human. Hyperparameters . Our training details are reported in Table 2. We fine-tune all models on one node server equipped with 900G CPU RAM and 8 A100 40G GPUs. Although single batch size is set in difference due to model size, we maintain a same global batch. Meanwhile, all other hyperparameters are set as the same. The optimizer is AdamW (Loshchilov and Hutter, 2019) and the scheduler is WarmupDecayLR (Goyal et al., 2017). We incorporate Deepspeed (Ren et al., 2021) and Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) to improve the training efficiency. Hardware consumption and time cost are reported as well. Although the training efficiency can be improved, we keep related parameters consistent with Alpaca and Vicuna (Taori et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023) for stable training.

texts.orig: Memo-usage Ability . We report results of tuning open-source LLMs to obtain memo-usage ability. Table 3 coincides the conclusion that larger LLMs are better zero- and few-shot learners (Brown et al., 2020). Largest ChatGPT and GPT4 models show a strong zero-shot inference ability, reaching over 60 and 50 scores on retrieval and chatting tasks, respectively. In contrast, smaller LLMs cannot fully understand prompts, especially the dummy templates such as 'N#M' in 'Memo Retrieval'. Among open-source LLMs, it is clearly that larger models have better initial understanding towards new instructions, although the performance gap keeps in a low level range from 0.3 to 4.0 F 1 ∗ and F 1 # scores on 'Memo Writing' and 'Retrieval'.

texts.orig: To check the learning ability of open-source LLMs, two groups of settings are taken. The 10% and 100% training groups refer to fine-tune LLMs with 1k and 10k samples, respectively. It can be observed that, with only 1k samples, larger LLMs MemoChat-Vicuna-13B and -33B can familiarize the instructions better than smaller LLMs MemoChat-Fastchat-T5-3B and -Vicuna-7B, raising significant performance gaps by 15 ∼ 30 scores on F 1 ∗ and F 1 # in 'Memo Writing'. Also, except for 'Memo Retrieval', MemoChat-Vicuna13B maintains similar performance to -33B in this setting, which may be due to insufficient training. When training set is scaled to 10k, the largest MemoChat-Vicuna-33B has the most potential to push highest scores over all tasks. In general, all models fully fine-tuned with 10k training data show better performance than ChatGPT/GPT4, and their performances are gradually improved when model scale increases. The only reversal happens on MemoChat-Vicuna-7B and -T5-3B, where -7B gets lower scores on summary generation and retrieval.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Response Consistency . As in Table 3, MemoChatVicuna-13B and -33B are able to reach similar zeroshot performance of ChatGPT and GPT4 fine-tuned with 1k instruction. Thus, we suppose MemoChatVicuna-13B (1k), -33B (1k) and all MemoChat (10k) models have obtained self-use ability of on-the-fly memos, and to be involved in consistency testing. We report results of response con- sistency checking in Table 4: (1) Compared with previous baselines mostly focusing on persona or user portrait summary, MemoChat guides the chatbots to produce a general summary, which significantly improves the downstream performance by 7.0 ∼ 19.5 score on average, as shown by the results of MPC-ChatGPT, MemoryBank-ChatGPT and MemoChat-ChatGPT; (2) Except for T5-3B, all fine-tuned MemoChat models show better performance than ChatGPT-2k by 2.4 to 9.7 scores on average, proving the effectiveness of reconstructed instructions; (3) When the LLMs are not fully fine-tuned, MemoChat-Vicuna-13B (1k) even show higher performance than -33B version by 3.3 score on average. The less-finetuned -13B model gets a pretty good score 64.78 on the retrospection questions, due to its tendency to generate short answers and thus memorize more. (4) When all open-source LLMs are fully fine-tuned, the average performance is improved as model scale grows. However, the performances on conjunction questions remain similar. This may be due to the limitation of long-form question answering ability, while ChatGPT is an expert (Liu et al., 2023a).
texts.text: Response Consistency . As in Table 3, MemoChatVicuna-13B and -33B are able to reach similar zeroshot performance of ChatGPT and GPT4 fine-tuned with 1k instruction. Thus, we suppose MemoChatVicuna-13B (1k), -33B (1k) and all MemoChat (10k) models have obtained self-use ability of on-the-fly memos, and to be involved in consistency testing. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Case Analysis . We report a specific case in Figure 4. The case starts with discussions on ' Machine Learning ' and ' Antitrust Laws ' in first 8 turns. After over 7 turns of other topics, we raise two long-range questions. The first retrospection question asks about the number of ' types of machine learning ', which requires to recall relevant ' Machine Learning ' topics and accurately count the term number in the first local question. ChatGPT2k can not correctly answer the question due to text window limits, while both MemoChat -based chatbots work. The second question belongs to conjunction type that cross-topic retrieval and understanding are required. ChatGPT-2k completely misinterpretes it. While MemoChat-ChatGPT precisely links antitrust laws to machine learning. The MemoChat-Vicuna-33B (10k) correctly identifies that the ' Sherman Antitrust Act ', the specific name of an antitrust law, applies to the machine learning industry, although it incorrectly states ' the European Commission fined Google ' under the Sherman Act which is an American legislation, with the European Commission operating instead under EU law. Thus, GPT4 scores ChatGPT-2k, MemoChatChatGPT, and MemoChat-Vicuna-33B (10k), with 1, 100, and 90, respectively.

texts.orig: 5 Conclusion
texts.text: 5 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We proposed MemoChat , an instruction tuning pipeline to teach LLMs to self-use on-the-fly structured memos towards consistent long-range open-domain conversations, which can be decomposed as ' memorization-retrieval-response ' loops. We carefully designed three trainable instructions based on public datasets for structured writing, fast retrieval, and interations with memos. An expertannotated downstream evaluation set was curated and provided. Experiments on three testing scenarios and various LLM-based chatbots demonstrated the effectiveness of MemoChat .

Any in-distribution values such as line number in 'Memo Writing' or option id in 'Memo Retrival' may lead to a shortcut that LLMs can directly cope the values to meet format requirement, while dummy variables will push the models to understand prompts indeed. Sub-figures

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (1a) and (1b) illustrate 'Prompt Copy' obstacle in 'Memo Writing' and 'Memo Retrieval', respectively. The replacement of dummy inputs 'M' and 'N' with actual numbers can obviously lead to reasonable generation. The second challenge lies in 'Catastrophic Forgetting', which refers to the response collapse if all types of instructions are not balanced. As shown in the Sub-figure (2), when the scale of 'Chat w/ Memo' data is significantly smaller than the others, the chat response will be meaningless repetition. While such ill generation can be significantly eliminated once sufficient chatting corpus are supplemented. When is comes to the third challenge 'Prompt Misplacement', we supposed that the most important component of prompt should be placed to the end of prompt. Otherwise, the LLMs tend to do natural text generation instead of memo-related tasks. The Sub-figure (3) shows that when detailed task explanation is placed in front of task content, in 'Memo Writing', the chatbot does unexpected conversation continuation.

The replacement of dummy inputs 'M' and 'N' with actual numbers can obviously lead to reasonable generation. The second challenge lies in 'Catastrophic Forgetting', which refers to the response collapse if all types of instructions are not balanced. As shown in the Sub-figure (2), when the scale of 'Chat w/ Memo' data is significantly smaller than the others, the chat response will be meaningless repetition. While such ill generation can be significantly eliminated once sufficient chatting corpus are supplemented. When is comes to the third challenge 'Prompt Misplacement', we supposed that the most important component of prompt should be placed to the end of prompt. Otherwise, the LLMs tend to do natural text generation instead of memo-related tasks. The Sub-figure (3) shows that when detailed task explanation is placed in front of task content, in 'Memo Writing', the chatbot does unexpected conversation continuation.

Please read, memorize, and understand Task Conversation, then complete the task under the guidance of Task Introduction. ``` Task Conversation (line 1) user: Anna just email to say that the managers meeting is put off till next Monday. Will you have everything ready by then, Sabrina? Hey Sabrina, what's wrong? (line 2) bot: I'm so worried. I haven't heard from my sister for 2 weeks. (line 3) user: How often do you call each other? (line 4) bot: Normally at least once a week. But she's now a volunteer teacher in a mountain village in Africa. I can only write her. (line 5) user: The Mail can be really slow sometimes. I'm sure you'll hear from her soon. (line 6) bot: I hope so. ··· (line 14) bot: It'll take about 20 minutes. (line 15) user: The streets are heavy with traffic at this time of a day, are they? (line 16) bot: Yes, they are. (line 17) user: Is it the rush hour? (line 18) bot: Yes, it is. Are you in a hurry, sir? (line 19) user: No, I'm not. Would you please drive slowly and carefully? (line 20) bot: Yes, sir. ```

texts.orig: bot: Yes, the law we discussed, the Sherman Antitrust Act, applies to the machine learning industry. The Sherman Antitrust Act prohibits anti-competitive behavior, such as price-fixing, market allocation, and monopolization. In the machine learning industry, there have been examples of companies collaborating to fix prices or engaging in other anti-competitive behavior, which would be in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act. For example, in 2010, several technology companies, including Apple, Google, and Adobe, were accused of conspiring to suppress wages by agreeing not to poach each other's employees. This agreement was found to be in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act, and the companies were forced to abandon the agreement and pay a settlement. Additionally, in 2018, the European Commission fined Google $5 billion for violating the Sherman Antitrust Act by engaging in anti-competitive behavior with its Android operating system. The Commission found that Google had required mobile device manufacturers to pre-install its search engine and Chrome browser on their devices, which had the effect of stifling competition from other search engines and browsers.
